steps.title: Identify the relationship between the two sentences
steps.content: The first sentence establishes a cause: Kelley conducted extensive research while specializing in the history of segregation. The second sentence states an effect: colleagues regarded her book as a reliable source. The transition must express that the second outcome directly results from the first.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice, then confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Likewise,’: Signals that a parallel or similar point follows, not that one thing caused another. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Consequently,’: Signals that what follows is a direct result of what came before — Kelley’s extensive research caused colleagues to trust her book. ✓
  • Choice C — ‘Next,’: Signals a sequence in time or steps, not a cause-and-effect relationship. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘Nevertheless,’: Signals a contrast or concession, implying the second sentence contradicts or surprises relative to the first — the opposite of what the passage says. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
